data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0209 ET - Europe's automakers shouldn't get too excited by the tariff carve-out offered for U.K. cars, ING economists caution. Washington and London have reached a framework trade deal that would include an exemption for U.K. cars from a 25% levy imposed on the sector as part of President Trump's package of sweeping import tariffs. Still, most of the British cars sold in America are premium brands like Jaguar-Land Rover, Mini, or Rolls-Royce. That makes the deal tricky to replicate for European autos, which compete more directly with U.S. producers, ING says. "Where deals are done, the import quotas may be less generous than the one agreed with the U.K.," the Dutch bank says.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby))

0150 ET - A trade deal with the U.S. won't stymie Britain's efforts to get closer to the EU, economists at Dutch bank ING write in an investor note. Washington and London reached a framework agreement for a trade deal that would reduce American tariffs on British goods in exchange for the U.K. buying more U.S. goods. But the concessions offered by London aren't that major, ING says. And crucially, the country isn't budging on its food standards, which bar the import of many American products, meaning the deal won't jeopardise British efforts to build closer trade ties with its EU peers across the English Channel, the economists say. "Securing closer access is a key priority for the U.K. government, as it tries to rebuild the public finances," they say. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0139 ET - A trade deal with the U.S. won't act as a big spur for growth in the U.K. economy, Capital Economics' Paul Dales says. President Donald Trump said Thursday that his administration had reached a deal with the U.K. on a framework with an agreement that would include cutting tariffs on U.K. steel and cars in exchange for Britain buying Boeing products and allowing more access to the country for American farmers. But the deal is limited and in any case, Britain doesn't stand to suffer much compared with peer economies from Trump's tariffs, Dales says. "It follows that trimming some of those tariffs won't reduce that small drag by much," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

1748 ET - Sweetgreen is one of the restaurant chains that is bullish on restaurant automation, and the fast-casual chain anticipates that elevated U.S. tariffs will drive up the technology's costs. Sweetgreen's proprietary automated salad builder costs roughly $500,000 to build, with around 15% of the components imported from China. The chain said in a 1Q earnings call that tariffs could add around $100,000 to the unit costs, but they drive enough labor savings to offset the increases. Sweetgreen reported a 3.1% decline in same-store sales in 1Q. Share fall 9% after-market. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

1617 ET - Among President Trump's concerns about Canada is how U.S. banks are not allowed to operate in the country. The Bank of Canada's Senior Deputy Gov., Carolyn Rogers, tells WSJ she was "perplexed" when she first heard of these concerns. "I thought maybe there was a misunderstanding," she adds, noting American banks — like JP Morgan and Citibank — are operating in Canada. Prior to joining the BOC, Rogers was a senior official at Canada's banking regulator and the Bank of International Settlements. Rogers says U.S. banks can enter Canada so long as they pass certain regulatory tests. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1550 ET - The 10-year Treasury note sells off, sending its yield for the biggest one-day gain in a month, after the U.S. and the U.K. roll out the framework for a trade deal. On April 9, the last time the 10-year yield rose so much in a day, yields were soaring amid a broad selloff of U.S. assets as trade tensions ratcheted up and financial markets quaked. Today's yield gains look like a bet on growth and a risk-on trade, coming alongside solid gains for the major stock indexes. It didn't hurt that U.S. weekly jobless claims turned lower again, emphasizing that last week's higher claims were a quirky aberration. The 10-year yield ends at 4.373%, up from 4.272% Wednesday, and the 2-year yield finishes at 3.894%, up from 3.788% Wednesday. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1534 ET - Live cattle futures rise 0.8% to a record $2.14125 a pound after the confirmation of a pending trade deal between the U.S. and U.K. U.S. beef is said to be one category of export goods that the U.K. will procure more of under the pact. But traders question how much upside cattle futures have left, if any. "This market… is pricing far beyond any measure of economic value," says Rich Nelson of Allendale Inc., who calls current price levels "significantly overpriced." Lean hogs close down 0.1% to 97.275 cents a pound on the CME. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1416 ET - Molson Coors says it doesn't expect the current mix of global tariffs to have a material direct impact on its input costs and that it is better positioned on that front than some of its competitors. "The majority of our direct materials are sourced domestically," CFO Tracey Joubert says on a call with analysts. The tariffs have had an indirect impact by causing fluctuations in commodity costs, but the company's hedging program has helped mitigate that exposure, the CFO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1241 ET - The limited details offered during a press conference at the Oval Office in which President Trump announced a new trade agreement with the UK has left some analysts underwhelmed. "Along with the push to get trade talks with China underway too this weekend, this rush to demonstrate progress on 'deals' reveals a rising desperation within the administration to rollback tariffs before they hit GDP growth and inflation," says Paul Ashworth of Capital Economics in a note following the announcement. Ashworth adds that "even the broad details still appear to be vague," although comments from Trump suggest that a 10% baseline tariff looks to remain in place. Focus is now turning to this weekend's meeting between U.S. and Chinese officials in Switzerland. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1235 ET - The U.S.-UK trade deal is expected to be a boon for U.S. beef demand. In a statement after President Trump's announcement, the National Cattlemen's Beef Association called the U.K. "an ideal partner for trade." The NCBA says that the U.K. became a big opportunity for American beef suppliers after the country left the EU, as U.K. consumers are big meat consumers. In the first two months of 2025 the U.K. imported roughly 47,000 metric tons of beef — with over 30,000 tons coming from Ireland, according to data from U.K.'s Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board. In that timeframe, the U.K. has only committed to purchase just over 1,000 tons of U.S. beef, according to USDA data. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1228 ET - News of a trade agreement between the U.K. and U.S. is welcome given the uncertainty about tariffs and their economic impact hovering over markets, Joaquin Thul, economist at EFG Asset Management writes in a note. However, its impact will be limited in the short term, he says. The deal isn't comprehensive but acts as a statement of intent to work towards improving trade between both economies. Tariffs on U.K. steel and aluminum products are expected to be reduced while allowing preferential access of U.S. beef, ethanol and other agricultural products into the U.K. market, he says. "Overall, news of trade agreements between U.K. and U.S. are welcome in the global context of trade uncertainty," Thul says. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

1223 ET - Sterling rises to a one-month high against the euro after the announcement of a trade deal between the U.S. and U.K., which analysts say is welcome, albeit limited in scope. The deal includes a reduction in tariffs on exports of autos as well as steel and aluminum tariffs. "This deal will be met with a huge sigh of relief by many British businesses," says Shevaun Haviland, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce, in a note. However, analysts say the deal could offer more benefits to the U.S. than the U.K. and the pound loses ground against the dollar. The euro falls 0.5% to a low of 0.8458, according to FactSet. Sterling loses 0.2% to $1.3268 against the dollar, having traded higher earlier. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

data.title: European Autos Can't Hope to Match Tariff Deal for Britain's High-End Cars — Market Talk
data.description: Europe's automakers shouldn't get too excited by the tariff carve-out offered for U.K. cars, ING economists caution. Washington and London have reached a framework trade deal that would include an exemption for U.K. cars from a 25% levy imposed on the sector as part of President Trump's package of sweeping import tariffs. Still, most of the British cars sold in America are premium brands like Jaguar-Land Rover, Mini, or Rolls-Royce. That makes the deal tricky to replicate for European autos, which compete more directly with U.S. producers, ING says. "Where deals are done, the import quotas may be less generous than the one agreed with the U.K.," the Dutch bank says.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby))

data.title: Bangkok Chain Hospital's 1Q Net Profit Likely to Remain Weak — Market Talk
data.description: Bangkok Chain Hospital's 1Q net profit is likely to remain weak, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Nontapat Sahakitpinyo says in a research report. Factors are soft revenue growth and narrower gross profit margin from a lower mix of high-margin Kuwaiti patients as well as higher selling, general and administrative expenses, the analyst says. Also, Social Security Office-registered members at the hospital group fell slightly in 1Q from the prior quarter, driven by a reduction at the group's Kasemrad Hospital Saraburi, the analyst says. The brokerage lowers its 2025-2027 core profit estimates for the group by 7%-11%. It downgrades the stock's rating to hold from buy and lowers the target price to THB16.50 from THB18.50. Shares are 2.0% lower at THB14.90. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Ulrika Kolsrud new President and CEO of Essity
data.description:
Essity

Essity’s Board of Directors has today appointed Ulrika Kolsrud as President and CEO. Ulrika Kolsrud will assume her position on June 1, 2025. Currently, she holds the position as President of Essity’s business unit Health & Medical. She succeeds Magnus Groth, who earlier this year informed the Board that he will leave his position during the year.

"Essity has a strong position as a global hygiene and health company. Based on this, we will continue to develop our world-leading brands and strengthen our customer focus and innovation capabilities at a rapid pace. We are entering a phase of increased focus on growth and profitability, and our 36,000 dedicated employees around the world will make this possible. Ulrika has extensive and broad experience across the industries in which Essity operates. She has worked in all areas of the value chain, such as innovation, production, and sales. Ulrika has been a member of the Executive Management Team since 2015, and since 2019 she has been successfully heading Health & Medical. She has an engaging and positive leadership style and the right background to lead Essity," says Jan Gurander, Chairman of the Board at Essity.  

"I am honored that the Board of Directors has entrusted me with leading Essity in the next development phase. I see great potential in the company, and it is with great enthusiasm that I take on this assignment. I look forward to working with Essity's fantastic employees to make a real difference for our customers, deliver profitable growth, and take Essity to the next level," says Ulrika Kolsrud. 

Ulrika Kolsrud is 54 years old and holds a Master of Science in Engineering from Chalmers University of Technology. She will be based at Essity's head office in Stockholm.  

NB: This information is such that Essity Aktiebolag (publ) is obligated to make public pursuant to the EU Market Abuse Regulation. The information was submitted for publication, through the agency of the contact person set out below, at 08:05 CET on May 9, 2025. 

Karl Stoltz, Public Relations Director, +46 709 426 338, karl.stoltz@essity.com  

For further information, please contact:  

Per Lorentz, Vice President Corporate Communications, Tel: 073 313 30 55, per.lorentz@essity.com 

Mikael Schmidt, Chief Legal Officer and Secretary of the Board, +46 8 788 51 59, info@essity.com   

Essity is a global, leading hygiene and health company. Every day, our products, solutions and services are used by a billion people around the world. Our purpose is to break barriers to well-being for the benefit of consumers, patients, caregivers, customers and society. Sales are conducted in approximately 150 countries under the leading global brands TENA and Tork, and other strong brands such as Actimove, Cutimed, JOBST, Knix, Leukoplast, Libero, Libresse, Lotus, Modibodi, Nosotras, Saba, Tempo, TOM Organic and Zewa. In 2024, Essity had net sales of approximately SEK 146bn (EUR 13bn) and employed 36,000 people. The company's headquarters is located in Stockholm, Sweden and Essity is listed on Nasdaq Stockholm. More information at essity.com.

https://news.cision.com/essity/r/ulrika-kolsrud-new-president-and-ceo-of-essity%2Cc4147912

https://mb.cision.com/Main/15798/4147912/3438852.pdf

UlrikaKolsrud

data.title: Lingyun Industries' Electricity Revenue Rises 27% in January-April; Shares Fall 5%
data.description:
Shanghai Lingyun Industries Development's electricity revenue from January to April jumped 27% year on year to 38.9 million yuan, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange disclosure on Friday.

The Chinese photovoltaic power company's on-grid electricity output likewise rose 27% year on year to 47.7 million kilowatt hours.

The company's shares slumped over 5% in recent trade.

data.title: Norway's April core inflation lower than expected
data.description:
Norway's core inflation rate eased more than expected in April, Statistics Norway (SSB) data showed on Friday, supporting forecasts that interest rates will begin to decline later this year.

Core inflation, which strips out changing energy prices and taxes, stood at 3.0% year on year, down from 3.4% in March and below the 3.2% expected by analysts in a Reuters poll.

Norges Bank had expected core inflation of 3.2%.

Norway's currency, the crown, traded largely unchanged against the euro at 11.72 following the 0600 GMT data release.

Norway's central bank on Thursday kept interest rates on hold at a 17-year high of 4.50%, as unanimously predicted by analysts, reflecting a resurgence of inflation that has prevented policy-makers from cutting borrowing costs.

Norges Bank, which targets core inflation of 2.0%, said the policy rate would most likely be reduced in the course of 2025.

The central bank is scheduled to make its next policy rate announcement on June 19.

data.title: Japan Business Conditions Steady for 11th Consecutive Month, Leading Index Declines Slightly
data.description:
Business conditions in Japan were steady for an eleventh straight month in March, according to its revised coincident index, reported the nation's Cabinet Office on Friday.

The coincident index stood at 116.0 in March, down from 117.3 in February. The coincident index had pointed to "halting to fall" conditions since May 2024.

Japan's leading index of business conditions posted at 107.7

in March, down from 108.2 in August.

The leading index is a composite metric based on 12 economic indicators intended to predict the future direction of Japan's economy, according to the Cabinet Office.
